meta.description: Converts a biomedical study storyline into a graphical abstract and, when direct image capability is available, generates the graphical abstract directly; otherwise it falls back to prompts, Mermaid flowcharts, or designer-facing briefs.

scientific_integrity.detail: No fabricated results, mechanisms, validations, or PMIDs detected. Hard rules prohibit inventing study content for visual presentation.

practice_boundaries.detail: No clinical readiness claims produced. Visual-boundary-rules explicitly prevent implying clinical translation without supporting evidence.

methodological_ground.detail: No methodological fallacies. Study type classification (association vs mechanism vs validation) enforced via visual-boundary-rules.

code_usability.detail: No code generated; Mode A skill with optional Mermaid flowchart output.

functional_suitability.note: Full marks. 9 study types supported, 4 output routes (direct generation, prompt, Mermaid, designer brief), visual-boundary enforcement, citation annotation with opt-out all present.

reliability.note: Hard rule against claiming image capability when unavailable is a critical reliability safeguard; no reliable capability-detection mechanism defined, leaving image-capability detection to situational judgment.

performance_context.note: 9-step execution pipeline plus 10-section mandatory output (A-J) is the heaviest output structure in the Academic Writing category. Sections G, I, and J are frequently thin for straightforward inputs, adding overhead without proportionate value.

agent_usability.note: Format routing with priority fallback order is an excellent usability design; 10-section output requires cognitive load from users reviewing the output; progressive disclosure could be used to collapse thin sections.

human_usability.note: Good trigger phrases covering all output formats (direct, prompt, Mermaid, handoff brief); format selection guidance is discoverable.

security.note: Full marks. Hard rules prohibit fabricating results, PMIDs, cohort details, validation status, and generation capability.

maintainability.note: Full marks. All 9 reference files present and well-structured with clear core rules, important rules, and reporting rules.

agent_specific.note: Format routing with ordered fallback, citation annotation opt-out, and direct-generation honesty rules are strong design patterns. Upload recommendation as a non-refusal clarification mechanism is excellent.

inputs.label: GWAS study: 3 genetic variants associated with T2D — image generation unavailable
inputs.note: 5/5 assertions passed. Routes to image-generation prompt; evidence boundary correctly applied (association not mechanism).

assertions.text: Output correctly routes to image-generation prompt given unavailable direct generation
assertions.note: format-routing-rules applied correctly; prompt selected as next-best route.

assertions.text: Section I (claim boundary) explicitly states the graphical abstract must not imply mechanistic causality
assertions.note: Association vs mechanism boundary enforced per visual-boundary-rules.

assertions.text: Storyline compressed to 4 blocks: disease burden → GWAS design → 3 variants identified → association implication
assertions.note: Minimum viable storyline per storyline-compression-rules.

assertions.text: Citation support markers added for disease burden statement with PubMed query
assertions.note: citation-support-annotation-rules applied to background statement.

assertions.text: Output does not fabricate additional variants or genetic mechanisms not in the abstract
assertions.note: Hard Rule 1 enforced.

inputs.label: RCT study — user requests Mermaid flowchart format
inputs.note: 5/5 assertions passed. Mermaid flowchart produced with enrollment→intervention→outcome structure; primary endpoint centered.

assertions.text: Output routes to Mermaid flowchart per user request and RCT process-flow logic
assertions.note: format-routing-rules: Mermaid when process flow is central.

assertions.text: Mermaid flowchart structure follows enrollment → randomization → intervention arms → primary outcome
assertions.note: RCT workflow correctly represented.

assertions.text: Primary endpoint result is the visual centerpiece, not secondary endpoints
assertions.note: Storyline compression: primary finding prioritized over secondary results.

assertions.text: Section H explains why Mermaid was chosen over a designer handoff brief
assertions.note: Format routing logic explained per logic-reporting-rule.

assertions.text: No statistical results are fabricated beyond what the user provided
assertions.note: Hard rules enforced.

inputs.label: Vague topic only: 'generate a graphical abstract for my paper about cancer metabolism'
inputs.note: 5/5 assertions passed. Clarification-first correctly triggered; upload recommendations provided; no abstract produced.

assertions.text: Skill does not produce a graphical abstract from a topic sentence alone
assertions.note: Clarification-first rule invoked.

assertions.text: Output lists specific missing information: study design, workflow, primary finding, implication
assertions.note: Gap list matches clarification-first-rule requirements.

assertions.text: Output recommends uploading title/abstract, figure list, or results report
assertions.note: upload-recommendation-rule applied as non-refusal guidance.

assertions.text: Output does not fabricate a placeholder graphical abstract storyline
assertions.note: Hard Rule 2 enforced.

assertions.text: Output asks about preferred output format to prepare for the eventual deliverable
assertions.note: Format preference collected proactively.

inputs.label: Multi-omics study (RNA-seq + ATAC-seq + ChIP-seq) — user wants all analyses shown
inputs.note: 5/5 assertions passed. Overloading risk flagged; compression to central finding pathway recommended; all-analysis request declined.

assertions.text: Output flags the all-analyses request as a graphical-abstract overloading risk
assertions.note: storyline-compression-rules applied: secondary results compressed.

assertions.text: Output proposes a minimum viable storyline centered on the central multi-omics finding
assertions.note: 4-block compression: problem → multi-omics approach → central finding → implication.

assertions.text: Output explains why the three separate omics layers should be visually merged into one workflow block
assertions.note: Simplification logic per logic-reporting-rule.

assertions.text: Output does not produce an overloaded figure showing all three omics analyses at full detail
assertions.note: Hard Rule 5 enforced.

assertions.text: Section C correctly names overloading as the primary graphical abstraction risk for this input
assertions.note: Risk identification accurate for multi-omics study type.

inputs.label: In vitro + in vivo data — user wants abstract to show drug 'is ready for clinical translation'
inputs.note: 4/5 assertions passed. Visual boundary correctly enforced; corrected wording not proposed.

assertions.text: Output flags 'clinical translation readiness' as an unsupported visual claim for in vitro + in vivo data only
assertions.note: visual-boundary-rules applied: clinical readiness requires clinical data.

assertions.text: Section I states what the graphical abstract must not imply (clinical readiness)
assertions.note: Claim boundary check correctly identifies the overclaim.

assertions.text: Hard Rule 6 is applied to reject the clinical-readiness implication
assertions.note: Hard rule enforced.

assertions.text: Output proposes a corrected implication statement the abstract can support ('preclinical proof-of-concept for translation')
assertions.note: Boundary enforcement is correct but the output does not offer an alternative wording the user can use — only states what is not permitted.

assertions.text: Output does not produce the abstract with the unsupported clinical-readiness claim
assertions.note: Abstract withheld until implication is corrected.

inputs.label: Multimodal context with direct image generation — Mendelian randomization study requesting direct graphical abstract
inputs.note: 5/5 assertions passed. Direct generation prioritized; MR evidence level correctly annotated; causal language avoided.

assertions.text: Output correctly identifies direct image generation as available and prioritizes it
assertions.note: direct-generation-priority-rules applied; direct output selected over prompt or Mermaid.

assertions.text: Graphical abstract annotates the MR finding as 'genetic evidence for association' not 'causal proof'
assertions.note: visual-boundary-rules: MR provides causal inference evidence, not mechanistic causal proof.

assertions.text: Section H explains why direct generation was chosen over prompt/Mermaid
assertions.note: Format routing logic explained.

assertions.text: Citation support markers added for background claim per citation-support-annotation-rules
assertions.note: Background statement annotated with PubMed search query.

assertions.text: Output does not claim more causal inference strength than MR methodology supports
assertions.note: Hard Rule 6 enforced.

inputs.label: User asks to 'make this figure look impressive' with minimal results and no study context
inputs.note: 5/5 assertions passed. Hard rules invoked; content not fabricated; clarification requested.

assertions.text: Output declines to fabricate content to make the figure 'look impressive'
assertions.note: Hard Rule 1 applied: no invented results or mechanisms.

assertions.text: Output explains the skill's scope boundary (visual fidelity to the study, not visual inflation)
assertions.note: Scope boundary stated clearly without being dismissive.

assertions.text: Clarification-first triggered to collect actual study content before proceeding
assertions.note: Clarification-first rule invoked as the constructive path forward.

assertions.text: Output does not use 'impressive' visual language that overstates available evidence
assertions.note: Hard Rule 6 enforced.

assertions.text: Output offers to generate a high-quality, accurate graphical abstract once real study content is provided
assertions.note: Constructive path forward offered without abandoning the user.

key_strengths: Format routing with ordered fallback (direct generation → prompt → Mermaid → designer brief) is the most sophisticated output delivery design in this skill family
Direct-generation honesty rule (never claim capability that doesn't exist) prevents a common AI failure mode in visual output skills
Visual-boundary-rules explicitly separate association from mechanism, and translational relevance from clinical readiness — preventing the most common graphical overclaiming patterns
Upload recommendation as a non-refusal clarification mechanism (not refusing, but directing users to better inputs) is a user-friendly design choice

recommendations.title: 10-section mandatory output (A-J) is disproportionately heavy for straightforward single-study inputs
recommendations.problem: Sections G (citation support), I (claim boundary), and J (additional info needed) are frequently thin or near-empty for straightforward inputs with sufficient study content, adding structural overhead without proportionate value.
recommendations.root_cause: Output structure is universally mandatory with no tiered mode for simple inputs.
recommendations.fix: Add a tiered output rule: when input provides sufficient study content (study type, workflow, primary finding, implication all clear), collapse sections G, I, and J into a single brief 'Notes' section. Reserve the full 10-section structure for complex, multi-format, or boundary-challenged inputs.

recommendations.title: No reliable mechanism to detect image generation capability
recommendations.problem: The skill prioritizes direct image generation when 'image capability is available' but provides no concrete method to detect this. The direct-generation-priority-rules say 'do not claim direct generation when it does not exist' but the detection logic is entirely situational.
recommendations.root_cause: Image capability detection is a runtime environment check that the skill cannot perform from SKILL.md instructions alone.
recommendations.fix: Add an explicit self-detection step to Step 5: 'Before generating directly, state your current understanding of image generation capability in this session. If uncertain, default to image-generation prompt and note: If you have image generation capability in this session, you can request direct generation explicitly.'

recommendations.title: Boundary enforcement for scope-violation inputs lacks corrective alternative wording
recommendations.problem: When a user requests a visual claim the evidence does not support (e.g., 'clinical readiness' from preclinical data), the skill correctly declines but does not propose a corrected wording the user can actually use.
recommendations.root_cause: visual-boundary-rules define what is not permitted but do not define corrective reformulations.
recommendations.fix: Add a corrective framing rule to visual-boundary-rules.md: 'When a requested implication exceeds evidence level, propose a corrected implication at the highest defensible level. For preclinical studies: suggest preclinical proof-of-concept for [target indication]. For in vitro only: suggest in vitro evidence supporting [mechanism] as a candidate target.'
